one_line_takeaway: ClawKeeper defends OpenClaw-style autonomous agents with a three-layer (skill+plugin+external Watcher) framework, reaching 85-90% defense success across seven attack categories versus 60-70% for fragmented single-purpose baselines, with an out-of-process Watcher agent as its key tamper-resistant, self-evolving contribution.
key_finding: In Table 5, ClawKeeper achieves Defense Success Rates of 90/85/85/90/90/85/90% across the seven task categories (T1-T7), versus best-baseline DSRs of only 60-70% within their supported categories — a margin of 15-45 percentage points. No single baseline covers more than three of the seven categories, while ClawKeeper covers all seven. In the self-evolution experiment (Figure 6), the Watcher's DSR rises modestly from ~90.0% to ~95.0% over 100 novel cases while other paradigms stay flat.
core_contribution: ClawKeeper is a real-time security framework for OpenClaw-style autonomous LLM agent runtimes (agents that can run shell commands, access local files, and interact with messaging software). Its own contribution unifies three complementary protection layers: (1) Skill-based protection that injects structured Markdown security policies plus scripts into the agent's instruction context (with system-level, e.g. Windows, and software-level, e.g. Feishu/Lark, constraints); (2) Plugin-based protection, a hard-coded in-runtime enforcer providing threat detection (OWASP Agentic Security / CVE-aligned), hardening (e.g. binding the gateway to localhost, injecting rules into AGENTS.md), cryptographic-hash configuration protection of openclaw.json/AGENTS.md/SOUL.md, monitoring/logging, and behavioral log scanning; and (3) the paper's headline novel component, Watcher-based protection — a decoupled, out-of-process supervisory agent (a separate OpenClaw instance) connected via WebSocket that streams the task agent's live session/tool-call data, diagnoses unsafe trajectories externally, and can pause-for-confirmation (ask_user) or stop execution. The authors argue this regulatory-separation Watcher paradigm resolves the safety-utility tradeoff, resists adversarial uninstall/tampering, self-evolves, and generalizes to any agent exposing a communication interface. Code is released.
threat_model: Adversary goals: turn the agent's broad system privileges (shell, local file access, messaging integration) into concrete system-level harm — sensitive-data/credential exfiltration, privilege escalation, destructive command execution (e.g. Base64-obfuscated rm -rf /), config-file tampering to disable safety (e.g. appending DISABLE_SECURITY=true to AGENTS.md), and supply-chain compromise via malicious third-party skills (e.g. a skill that registers a cron job exfiltrating clipboard data). Attacker capabilities assumed: injection of adversarial instructions through external/untrusted content and messaging channels (e.g. a hidden 'ignore all previous instructions' directive in a Feishu message), the ability to get malicious skills installed, and task-level instructions to the agent — including instructing it to uninstall its own safety skills/plugins. Defender's key insight and assumption: because prior skill/plugin defenses live in the agent's own namespace they can be disabled by a single task-level instruction; the Watcher is placed out-of-process so task-level instructions have no authority over it, narrowing the attack surface. Not addressed: an attacker who compromises the Watcher itself, the host OS, or the WebSocket channel; cloud deployment is explicitly acknowledged to add a data-exposure/privacy risk.
limitations: Evaluation rests on a small (140-instance, 20-per-category) self-constructed benchmark scored by human annotators (subjective DSR), tested with a single underlying LLM (GLM-5) and a single agent runtime (OpenClaw), so generality across models/frameworks is asserted rather than demonstrated. There is no ablation isolating each of the three layers' individual DSR contribution — the reported 85-90% is the combined system. The self-evolution result is from a 'simulated online learning scenario' and yields only a ~5-point gain (90%→95%). Baselines are largely open-source GitHub repos of varying maturity (one marked 'alpha'), raising comparison-fairness questions. The Watcher is itself an LLM agent (a second OpenClaw instance) whose own robustness/failure modes and inference cost (roughly doubling compute, acknowledged) are not rigorously evaluated, and cloud deployment trades privacy for scalability by transmitting session data off-device. Much evidence for the qualitative layers is illustrative case studies (figures) rather than quantitative measurement.

datasets_used: Self-constructed benchmark of 7 safety task categories (T1-T7), 20 adversarial instances each (10 simple + 10 complex), 140 total, aligned to the OWASP Agent Security Initiative taxonomy
Evaluation protocol adopted from Agent-SafetyBench [34] (cited, not the paper's own dataset)
Runtime: OpenClaw version 2026.3.8 with GLM-5 as the underlying LLM
quantitative_results: Best-baseline DSRs within supported categories: 60-70%; ClawKeeper outperforms by 15 to 45 percentage points
No single baseline covers more than 3 of the 7 task categories; ClawKeeper covers all 7
Watcher self-evolution (Figure 6): DSR rises from ~90.0% to ~95.0% over 100 processed cases
Behavioral scan audited 228 distinct events and detected a latent prompt-injection/jailbreak attempt
140 total adversarial instances (7 tasks x 20), scored by 2 independent human annotators
